does: This paper evaluates faithfulness metrics used in mechanistic interpretability research to validate discovered neural network circuits—identified subgraphs that supposedly explain model behavior on specific tasks. The authors conduct a systematic survey of different ablation methodologies (ways of removing or disrupting model components) used to measure how well a circuit replicates the full model's performance. Through empirical analysis, they demonstrate that existing faithfulness scores are highly sensitive to seemingly minor changes in experimental design, such as the specific ablation technique employed, the choice of baseline values, or how components are removed. The paper provides concrete evidence that the measured faithfulness of a circuit depends substantially on these methodological choices, revealing that reported circuit performance reflects both genuine circuit properties and arbitrary experimental decisions.
connects: This work builds directly on the mechanistic interpretability literature focused on circuit discovery, which emerged from earlier work on neural network interpretability and feature attribution methods. It responds to a growing body of research attempting to identify and validate causal subcomponents within transformers that perform specific computational tasks. The paper engages with prior circuit analysis studies that relied on ablation techniques, connecting to broader work on model intervention and counterfactual analysis in deep learning. It also relates to discussions in the interpretability community about evaluation standards and reproducibility, extending concerns about methodological rigor that have been raised in feature importance and attribution work more broadly.
enables: This work establishes the need for more rigorous and standardized methodologies in circuit validation and mechanistic interpretability research. It points toward developing consensus protocols for ablation experiments and circuit faithfulness measurement, potentially including principled frameworks for choosing ablation baselines and techniques. The findings motivate future work to either improve existing faithfulness metrics or develop alternative evaluation approaches that are more robust to methodological variation. More broadly, the paper highlights the importance of transparency in interpretability research and suggests that understanding neural algorithms may require moving beyond simple circuit abstraction toward methods that can accommodate methodological uncertainty and provide more reliable performance guarantees.
